content:
# Luke Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 110-111 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_018_page_001.jpg, LUK_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And he was speaking a parable to them about the [need] to always pray and not to lose-heart,

**2.** saying, "A certain judge was in a certain city, [one] not fearing God and not having-regard-for man.

**3.** And a widow was in that city, and she kept coming to him, saying, 'Avenge me from my adversary.'

**4.** And he was not willing for a time; but after these [things] he said within himself, 'Even-if I do not fear God nor have-regard-for man,

**5.** yet because this widow causes me trouble, I will avenge her, lest coming to-the-end she wear-me-out.'"

**6.** And the Lord said, "Hear what the judge of unrighteousness says.

**7.** And will not God bring-about the avenging of his chosen-ones, those crying-out to him day and night, and having-patience over them?

**8.** I say to you that he will bring-about their avenging quickly. Nevertheless, the Son of Man, having come, will he find faith upon the earth?"

**9.** And he spoke also to some who had-persuaded-themselves that they are righteous, and were despising the rest, this parable:

**10.** "Two men went-up into the temple to pray, the one a Pharisee and the other a tax-collector.

**11.** The Pharisee, having stood, was praying these [things] to himself: 'God, I thank you that I am not like the rest of men — extortioners, unrighteous, adulterers — or even like this tax-collector.

**12.** I fast twice [in] the week; I give-a-tenth [of] all that I acquire.'

**13.** But the tax-collector, having stood far-off, was not willing even [to] lift-up [his] eyes to heaven, but was striking his breast, saying, 'God, be-merciful to me, the sinner.'

**14.** I say to you, this [one] went-down to his house having-been-justified rather-than that [one]; because everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the [one] who humbles himself will be exalted."

**15.** And they were bringing to him also the infants, that he might touch them; but the disciples, having seen [this], were rebuking them.

**16.** But Jesus called them to [himself], saying, "Permit the little-children to come to me, and do not hinder them; for of such-ones is the kingdom of God.

**17.** Truly I say to you, whoever does not receive the kingdom of God as a little-child, shall by-no-means enter into it."

**18.** And a certain ruler asked him, saying, "Good Teacher, what having done shall I inherit eternal life?"

**19.** And Jesus said to him, "Why do you call me good? No one [is] good except one — God.

**20.** You know the commandments: 'Do not commit-adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear-false-witness, honor your father and mother.'"

**21.** And he said, "All these [things] I have kept from youth."

**22.** And having heard [this], Jesus said to him, "Still one [thing] is lacking to you: sell all, as-much-as you have, and distribute to [the] poor, and you will have treasure in heaven, and come, follow me."

**23.** But having heard these [things], he became exceedingly-sorrowful, for he was very wealthy.

**24.** And Jesus, having seen him, said, "How difficultly those who have riches will enter into the kingdom of God!

**25.** For it is easier [for] a camel to enter through [the] eye of a needle than [for] a rich-man to enter into the kingdom of God."

**26.** And those who heard [this] said, "And who is able to be saved?"

**27.** And he said, "The [things] impossible with men are possible with God."

**28.** And Peter said, "Behold, we have left our-own [things] and followed you."

**29.** And he said to them, "Truly I say to you, there is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for-the-sake-of the kingdom of God,

**30.** who shall not receive-back manifold-more in this time, and in the age [that is] coming, eternal life."

**31.** And having taken-aside the twelve, he said to them, "Behold, we are going-up to Jerusalem, and all the [things] that have been written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man will be completed.

**32.** For he will be handed-over to the nations, and will be mocked and will be insulted and will be spit-upon,

**33.** and having scourged [him], they will kill him, and [on] the third day he will rise-again."

**34.** And they understood none of these [things], and this word was hidden from them, and they were not knowing the [things] being said.

**35.** And it came-to-be, in his drawing-near to Jericho, a certain blind [man] was sitting beside the road, begging.

**36.** And having heard a crowd passing-through, he was inquiring what this might be.

**37.** And they reported to him that Jesus the Nazarene is passing-by.

**38.** And he cried-out, saying, "Jesus, Son of David, have-mercy-on me!"

**39.** And those going-before were rebuking him, that he should be-silent; but he was crying-out much more, "Son of David, have-mercy-on me!"

**40.** And having stood-still, Jesus commanded him to be brought to him. And [when] he drew-near, he asked him,

**41.** "What do you wish [that] I should do for you?" And he said, "Lord, that I might receive-sight."

**42.** And Jesus said to him, "Receive-sight; your faith has saved you."

**43.** And immediately he received-sight, and was following him, glorifying God. And all the people, having seen [it], gave praise to God.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **18:1 — "to always pray and not to lose-heart" (παντοτε προσευχεσθαι και μη ενκακειν/εγκακειν):** Sinaiticus reads παντοτε προσευχεσθαι αυτους και μη ενκακειν. The verb εγκακεω (or ενκακεω, "to lose-heart/become weary/give-up") appears in Paul's letters (2 Corinthians 4:1, 16; Galatians 6:9; Ephesians 3:13; 2 Thessalonians 3:13) but only here in the Gospels. It derives from εν + κακος ("in bad [condition]") — to become inwardly bad, to collapse spiritually. The infinitive construction (προσευχεσθαι...μη ενκακειν) with προς το ("toward the [end] that") establishes purpose: the parable is told specifically to teach the necessity of persistent prayer against the temptation to despair. The adverb παντοτε ("always/at all times") is emphatic — not merely "often" or "regularly" but without ceasing. This parable is directly connected to 17:22-37: the disciples who will long for the Son of Man's day and not see it must sustain prayer through the waiting.

2. **18:2-5 — "A certain judge...not fearing God" (κριτης τις...τον θεον μη φοβουμενος):** Sinaiticus reads κριτης τις ην εν τινι πολει τον θεον μη φοβουμενος και ανθρωπον μη εντρεπομενος. The judge is characterized by a double negative: he does not "fear God" (τον θεον μη φοβουμενος) — lacking piety — and does not "have-regard-for man" (ανθρωπον μη εντρεπομενος) — lacking shame or social conscience. The verb εντρεπω in the middle voice means "to turn oneself toward, to have regard for, to feel shame before." This judge is utterly insulated from both divine and human accountability. The widow (χηρα) represents the most vulnerable class in ancient society — without husband, without legal advocate, without power. Her only weapon is persistence: ηρχετο (imperfect, "she kept coming") indicates repeated, relentless approaches. The verb υπωπιαζη ("wear-me-out," v. 5) literally means "to strike under the eye/give a black-eye" — the judge fears being metaphorically beaten black-and-blue by her incessant coming. The phrase εις τελος ("to the end") can mean "finally/at last" or "without end/perpetually" — the judge dreads either her ultimate exhaustion of his patience or her endless assault.

3. **18:6-8 — "the judge of unrighteousness" (ο κριτης της αδικιας):** Sinaiticus reads ο κριτης της αδικιας — a Hebraic genitive of quality ("the unrighteous judge"). Jesus' argument is from the lesser to the greater (qal wahomer): if even an unrighteous, godless judge eventually yields to persistent pleading, how much more will the righteous God — who loves his chosen-ones (εκλεκτων, "elect/chosen") — bring about justice for them? The phrase βοωντων αυτω ημερας και νυκτος ("crying-out to him day and night") echoes the cries of the martyrs in Revelation 6:10. The verb μακροθυμει ("having-patience/being-long-tempered") is debated: does God exercise patience with his elect (delaying to test their faith) or with their oppressors (withholding judgment)? Both readings have support. The promise εν ταχει ("quickly/in speed") assures that God's response, though it may seem delayed, will be swift when it comes. The final question — πλην ο υιος του ανθρωπου ελθων αρα ευρησει την πιστιν επι της γης ("the Son of Man, having come, will he find faith upon the earth?") — is the most haunting question in Luke's Gospel. The article την before πιστιν ("the faith") is specific: not faith in general but the faith — the persistent, crying-out, never-giving-up faith of the parable. The particle αρα expresses anxious uncertainty. Jesus does not answer his own question.

4. **18:9 — "who had-persuaded-themselves that they are righteous" (τους πεποιθοτας εφ εαυτοις οτι εισιν δικαιοι):** Sinaiticus reads προς τινας τους πεποιθοτας εφ εαυτοις οτι εισιν δικαιοι. The perfect participle πεποιθοτας (from πειθω, "to persuade") indicates a settled, completed state of self-persuasion — they have convinced themselves and remain convinced. The preposition εφ εαυτοις ("upon themselves") indicates the basis of their confidence: it rests on themselves, not on God. They are simultaneously "despising the rest" (εξουθενουντας τους λοιπους) — the verb εξουθενεω ("to count as nothing/treat with contempt") is built from εξ + ουθεν ("out of nothing") — they regard others as nothing, as non-entities. The parable that follows is told precisely to expose and demolish this posture.

5. **18:10-14 — The Pharisee and the tax-collector:** Sinaiticus reads ανεβησαν ("went-up") — the temple stood on Mount Moriah, so one always "went up" to it. The Pharisee σταθεις ("having stood") is debated: does he stand conspicuously apart, or is this merely a note of posture? The phrase προς εαυτον ταυτα προσηυχετο ("was praying these [things] to himself") in Sinaiticus can be parsed two ways: (a) he stood by himself and prayed these things, or (b) he prayed these things to himself — his prayer never reaches God because its true audience is himself. His prayer catalogues virtues: fasting twice per week (δις του σαββατου — Mondays and Thursdays, beyond the single annual fast required by Torah on Yom Kippur) and tithing everything he acquires (αποδεκατω παντα οσα κτωμαι — including items not strictly requiring a tithe). The tax-collector (τελωνης) stands μακροθεν ("far-off") — at the margin of the temple court, as far from the holy presence as he can position himself. His posture is the opposite of the Pharisee's: he will not lift his eyes, he strikes his breast (ετυπτεν το στηθος αυτου — the imperfect indicating repeated self-striking), and his prayer is seven words in Greek: ο θεος ιλασθητι μοι τω αμαρτωλω ("God, be-propitiated/be-merciful to me, the sinner"). The verb ιλασκομαι ("to be propitious/to expiate") is a cultic term — the same root as ιλαστηριον ("mercy-seat/place of atonement"). The article τω before αμαρτωλω ("the sinner") is crucial: he does not say "a sinner" but "the sinner" — he sees himself as the sinner par excellence. The verdict δεδικαιωμενος ("having-been-justified," perfect passive participle) is a divine passive — God justified him. The comparative παρ εκεινον ("rather-than that [one]") indicates the tax-collector went down justified instead of the Pharisee.

6. **18:15-17 — "the infants" (τα βρεφη):** Sinaiticus reads τα βρεφη — not παιδια ("children") as in Mark 10:13 and Matthew 19:13, but βρεφη ("infants/babies"), a stronger word indicating very young children, even newborns (the same word used for the unborn John leaping in Elizabeth's womb, 1:41, and for the infant Jesus in the manger, 2:12, 16). The verb επετιμων ("were rebuking," imperfect) indicates the disciples repeatedly tried to stop the parents from bringing the infants — they considered this an inappropriate use of Jesus' time. Jesus' response uses two imperatives: αφετε ("permit/let go") and μη κωλυετε ("do not hinder," present imperative — stop the ongoing action of hindering). The phrase των τοιουτων ("of such-ones") in the clause "of such-ones is the kingdom of God" does not mean "of these children" specifically but "of ones like these" — those who share the qualities that infants represent: total dependence, inability to earn or achieve, receptivity without self-qualification.

7. **18:18 — "a certain ruler" (τις αρχων):** Sinaiticus reads αρχων τις — Luke alone identifies this questioner as an αρχων ("ruler/official"), which may indicate a synagogue ruler or a member of the local aristocracy. The question τι ποιησας ζωην αιωνιον κληρονομησω ("what having done shall I inherit eternal life?") contains a tension: κληρονομεω ("to inherit") implies receiving a gift (inheritance is not earned), but τι ποιησας ("what having done") implies a deed to be accomplished. The man frames salvation as both earned action and received inheritance — a confusion the encounter will expose.

8. **18:19 — "Why do you call me good?" (τι με λεγεις αγαθον;):** Sinaiticus reads τι με λεγεις αγαθον; ουδεις αγαθος ει μη εις ο θεος. Jesus does not deny being good; he interrogates the ruler's use of the word. If αγαθος ("good") in its absolute sense belongs to God alone, then calling Jesus "good teacher" is either flattery (using the word loosely) or a confession (recognizing something divine in him). The question forces the ruler to examine what he means.

9. **18:20 — The commandments listed:** Sinaiticus reads the commandments in this order: μη μοιχευσης ("do not commit-adultery"), μη φονευσης ("do not murder"), μη κλεψης ("do not steal"), μη ψευδομαρτυρησης ("do not bear-false-witness"), τιμα τον πατερα σου και την μητερα ("honor your father and mother"). This follows the Septuagint order of Deuteronomy 5:17-20 rather than the Hebrew order. Notably absent is the tenth commandment — "you shall not covet" — precisely the commandment most relevant to a very wealthy man. Jesus lists only the commandments governing human relationships (the "second table" of the Decalogue), omitting the first table (duties to God). The omission is strategic: the man's relationship with God will be tested in what follows.

10. **18:22 — "Still one [thing] is lacking to you" (ετι εν σοι λειπει):** Sinaiticus reads ετι εν σοι λειπει. The verb λειπω ("to lack/fall short") is present tense — the lack is current and ongoing. The command is fourfold: (1) παντα οσα εχεις πωλησον ("sell all, as-much-as you have") — aorist imperative, a decisive act; (2) διαδος πτωχοις ("distribute to [the] poor") — aorist imperative; (3) εξεις θησαυρον εν ουρανοις ("you will have treasure in heaven") — the promise; (4) δευρο ακολουθει μοι ("come, follow me") — the real demand. The selling and distributing are means to the end; the following is the end itself. The "one thing lacking" is not charity but total allegiance — the willingness to exchange earthly security for personal attachment to Jesus.

11. **18:23 — "he became exceedingly-sorrowful" (περιλυπος εγενετο):** Sinaiticus reads περιλυπος εγενηθη — the verb γινομαι in the aorist passive (εγενηθη) indicates something that happened to him rather than something he chose. The adjective περιλυπος (περι + λυπη, "surrounded by grief") describes grief that envelops completely. The explanatory clause ην γαρ πλουσιος σφοδρα ("for he was very wealthy") reveals the obstacle: his wealth was not merely a possession but an identity, and releasing it felt like annihilation. Unlike the tax-collector of the previous parable who had nothing to offer but his sin, this ruler has everything to offer but cannot let it go.

12. **18:24-25 — "How difficultly" (πως δυσκολως):** Sinaiticus reads πως δυσκολως. The adverb δυσκολως ("with difficulty/hardly") derives from δυσ- ("ill/hard") + κολον ("food") — originally "hard to please with food," then generalized to "difficult." The camel-and-needle saying (ευκοπωτερον γαρ εστιν καμηλον δια τρηματος βελονης εισελθειν, "it is easier for a camel to enter through an eye of a needle") is deliberately impossible imagery — not a "small gate" (a later, domesticating interpretation) but an actual sewing needle (βελονη, used only here in the NT; Luke the physician uses the precise term for a surgical needle) and an actual camel (καμηλος), the largest animal in Palestine. The point is absolute impossibility by human means — which is precisely the setup for verse 27.

13. **18:27 — "The [things] impossible with men are possible with God" (τα αδυνατα παρα ανθρωποις δυνατα παρα τω θεω εστιν):** Sinaiticus reads τα αδυνατα παρα ανθρωποις δυνατα παρα τω θεω εστιν. The adjective αδυνατα ("impossible/powerless") and δυνατα ("possible/powerful") share the same root δυναμις ("power"). The preposition παρα ("with/alongside") indicates the sphere of operation: what is impossible in the human sphere is possible in the divine sphere. This is not a softening of the camel saying but a theological resolution: salvation — for the rich or anyone — is never a human achievement but always a divine gift. The echo of Genesis 18:14 LXX (μη αδυνατει παρα τω θεω ρημα; "Is anything impossible with God?") is unmistakable.

14. **18:28 — "we have left our-own [things]" (ημεις αφηκαμεν τα ιδια):** Sinaiticus reads ημεις αφεντες τα ιδια ηκολουθησαμεν σοι. Peter's statement contrasts with the ruler who could not leave his possessions. The pronoun ημεις ("we") is emphatic — "we, at least, have done what he could not." The phrase τα ιδια ("our-own [things]/one's own possessions") is distinctively Lukan (cf. Acts 4:32) and encompasses everything that constituted their former lives — boats, nets, tax booth, homes, families.

15. **18:29-30 — "manifold-more in this time" (πολλαπλασιονα εν τω καιρω τουτω):** Sinaiticus reads πολλαπλασιονα εν τω καιρω τουτω. The adjective πολλαπλασιων ("manifold/many-times-over") is vague in its multiplication — Mark 10:30 specifies "hundredfold" (εκατονταπλασιονα), but Sinaiticus Luke uses the less specific "manifold-more." The list of things left — house (οικιαν), wife (γυναικα), brothers (αδελφους), parents (γονεις), children (τεκνα) — is comprehensive, covering property, marriage, siblings, ancestry, and posterity. The reward is twofold: πολλαπλασιονα εν τω καιρω τουτω ("manifold-more in this time") — the new community of faith provides what was surrendered — and εν τω αιωνι τω ερχομενω ζωην αιωνιον ("in the age [that is] coming, eternal life") — the ultimate inheritance the ruler originally sought.

16. **18:31-33 — The third passion prediction:** Sinaiticus reads τελεσθησεται παντα τα γεγραμμενα δια των προφητων τω υιω του ανθρωπου ("all the [things] that have been written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man will be completed"). The verb τελεω ("to complete/bring-to-an-end/fulfill") indicates not mere occurrence but purposeful completion — the events are the fulfillment of a prophetic script. This third prediction is the most detailed: παραδοθησεται ("he will be handed-over") to the εθνεσιν ("nations/Gentiles") — introducing the Roman involvement; εμπαιχθησεται ("will be mocked"), υβρισθησεται ("will be insulted/treated-shamefully"), εμπτυσθησεται ("will be spit-upon") — three verbs cataloguing humiliation; μαστιγωσαντες ("having scourged") and αποκτενουσιν ("they will kill") — the physical torment and death; and τη ημερα τη τριτη αναστησεται ("on the third day he will rise-again") — the reversal. Each detail will be fulfilled in chapters 22-24.

17. **18:34 — "they understood none of these [things]" (ουδεν τουτων συνηκαν):** Sinaiticus reads και αυτοι ουδεν τουτων συνηκαν. Luke provides a threefold statement of incomprehension: (1) ουδεν τουτων συνηκαν ("they understood none of these [things]") — intellectual failure; (2) ην το ρημα τουτο κεκρυμμενον απ αυτων ("this word was hidden from them") — divine concealment (the perfect passive participle κεκρυμμενον, from κρυπτω, "to hide," implies a divine agent); (3) ουκ εγινωσκον τα λεγομενα ("they were not knowing the [things] being said") — ongoing ignorance (imperfect tense). The threefold emphasis is unique to Luke and highlights that the disciples' failure to understand the passion is not merely intellectual dullness but involves a mysterious divine veiling that will only be lifted after the resurrection (cf. 24:25-27, 44-45).

18. **18:35 — "in his drawing-near to Jericho" (εν τω εγγιζειν αυτον εις Ιεριχω):** Sinaiticus reads εν τω εγγιζειν αυτον εις Ιεριχω. This creates a well-known discrepancy with Mark 10:46, which places the healing as Jesus was "going-out from Jericho" (εκπορευομενου αυτου απο Ιεριχω). Various harmonizations have been proposed (two Jerichos — the old and new city; two separate healings), but EMET simply translates what Sinaiticus reads. The blind man is unnamed in Luke; Mark identifies him as Bartimaeus son of Timaeus. The participle επαιτων ("begging") derives from επι + αιτεω ("to ask upon/to ask insistently") — he begged for his livelihood.

19. **18:38-39 — "Jesus, Son of David, have-mercy-on me" (Ιησου υιε Δαυειδ ελεησον με):** Sinaiticus reads Ιησου υιε Δαυειδ ελεησον με. The title "Son of David" (υιε Δαυειδ) is explicitly messianic — the blind man confesses what the sighted crowd merely reports (v. 37, "Jesus the Nazarene"). The verb εβοησεν ("he cried-out") indicates a loud, urgent shout. Those "going-before" (οι προαγοντες) — the vanguard of the crowd — rebuked him (επετιμων, imperfect — they kept rebuking), but he αυτος πολλω μαλλον εκραζεν ("himself was crying-out much more") — the imperfect εκραζεν indicates persistent, escalating cries. His persistence mirrors the widow of the opening parable: both refuse to be silenced, and both receive what they seek. The connection is deliberate — Luke frames the chapter with persistence rewarded.

20. **18:40-41 — "What do you wish [that] I should do for you?" (τι σοι θελεις ποιησω;):** Sinaiticus reads τι σοι θελεις ποιησω. Jesus' question is not for information — he knows what a blind beggar wants — but for confession: the man must articulate his need and his faith. The response κυριε ινα αναβλεψω ("Lord, that I might receive-sight") uses κυριε ("Lord") — a higher title than "Son of David," moving from messianic to divine address. The verb αναβλεπω ("to look-up/to see-again") contains the prefix ανα- ("again/up"), suggesting restoration of sight previously possessed.

21. **18:42-43 — "Receive-sight; your faith has saved you" (αναβλεψον· η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε):** Sinaiticus reads αναβλεψον· η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε. The imperative αναβλεψον ("receive-sight") and the perfect σεσωκεν ("has saved") echo precisely the declaration to the Samaritan leper in 17:19. The same formula — η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε — connects the two stories: the foreigner and the blind man both received salvation through persistent faith. The response is threefold: παραχρημα ανεβλεψεν ("immediately he received-sight") — instantaneous healing; ηκολουθει αυτω ("was following him") — imperfect tense indicating ongoing discipleship; δοξαζων τον θεον ("glorifying God") — worship as the natural response to healing. The crowd's response — πας ο λαος ιδων εδωκεν αινον τω θεω ("all the people, having seen [it], gave praise to God") — closes the chapter with universal praise, in contrast to the solitary gratitude of the Samaritan leper.

22. **18:1-43 — The structure of Luke 18:** The chapter is organized around the question of who enters the kingdom and how. The persistent widow (vv. 1-8) teaches that access to God requires relentless prayer. The Pharisee and tax-collector (vv. 9-14) reveal that access requires self-emptying humility, not self-congratulating virtue. The infants (vv. 15-17) demonstrate that access requires childlike receptivity. The rich ruler (vv. 18-30) exposes the obstacle of wealth and self-sufficiency. The passion prediction (vv. 31-34) reveals the cost of the kingdom to its King. The blind man (vv. 35-43) embodies all the chapter's themes: he is persistent (like the widow), humble (like the tax-collector), dependent (like the infants), and possessionless (unlike the ruler). His faith saves him — answering, at least in part, the haunting question of verse 8: yes, the Son of Man will find faith on the earth, but it will be found among the desperate, the marginalized, the blind, and the broken — those with nothing to offer except their cry.
